Image Compressor

Reduce image file size instantly. No quality loss, no uploads, no account needed.

Drop your image here

JPG, PNG or WebP. Runs entirely in your browser.

Maximum file size: 50MB
Need to resize a photo to exact dimensions?Try Image Resizer

Image compression is one of the most impactful optimizations you can make for website performance, email deliverability, and storage costs. Large image files slow down page loads, get rejected by upload forms, and consume unnecessary bandwidth. This tool lets you compress JPG, PNG, and WebP images directly in your browser, no upload, no account, and no waiting for a server to process your file. Whether you need to shrink a photograph for social media, hit a specific KB limit for a form, or batch-optimize images before a site launch, the Image Compressor gives you precise control in seconds.

How to use

  1. Upload your image. Drag a JPG, PNG, or WebP file onto the drop zone, or click to open your file browser. The preview loads immediately so you can see what you're working with before any compression is applied.
  2. Choose your compression method. Drag the Quality slider left to reduce file size (lower values mean smaller files with more compression). Alternatively, type a Target KB value, the tool will automatically find the highest quality level that keeps the output under your specified size.
  3. Review the result. The compressed preview and the new file size appear instantly. Compare it with the original size shown above to see how much you've saved before committing to the download.
  4. Download. Click the Download button to save the compressed image to your device. The output is a JPG file optimized for maximum compatibility across browsers, apps, and platforms.

Tips for best results

  • Start at 75–80% quality. For most photographs, 75–80% quality produces output that looks identical to the original on screen while cutting file size by 50–70%. Drop lower only if you need the smallest possible file.
  • Use Target KB for form uploads. If you're trying to meet a hard limit (many government forms cap attachments at 200 KB, 500 KB, or 1 MB), use the Target KB mode. It takes the guesswork out of hitting exact size requirements.
  • Compress before other edits. If you also need to resize the image or convert to a different format, compress last, resizing first reduces the amount of pixel data the compressor has to work with, giving you better results at the same quality level.
  • PNG vs JPG. PNG files with transparent backgrounds cannot be losslessly compressed to JPG without losing the transparency layer. If your PNG has a transparent background, use the image converter to choose your output format intentionally.

Why use PixMidas

  • 100% private. Compression runs entirely in your browser using the HTML5 Canvas API. Your image never leaves your device, there are no uploads, no cloud processing, and no third-party servers involved at any stage.
  • Two compression modes. Quality % gives you manual control over the size-quality trade-off. Target KB mode is ideal when you have a hard upload limit such as an email attachment restriction, a government form cap, or a CMS upload threshold.
  • No account needed. Open the tool and start compressing immediately, no signup, no watermark, no file count limits, and no expiry on downloads.

Frequently asked questions

Does compressing an image reduce quality?

Yes, but only perceptibly at aggressive settings. JPEG compression works by selectively discarding fine detail that the eye is unlikely to notice at normal viewing sizes. At 70–80% quality, most photographs look identical to the original on screen while being 50–70% smaller in file size. If you push below 50%, artifacts such as blockiness and color banding become visible, especially in smooth gradients and sky areas.

What image formats are supported?

JPG, JPEG, PNG, and WebP files are all accepted as input. The output is saved as a JPG, which produces the best balance of file size and universal compatibility across devices, browsers, email clients, and platforms. If you need a different output format, use the image converter after compression.

What does Target KB mode do exactly?

Instead of picking a quality percentage yourself, Target KB lets you specify the maximum file size you want in kilobytes. The tool runs an internal binary search, testing progressively lower quality levels, until it finds the highest quality that still keeps the output under your specified limit. This is much faster and more reliable than manually adjusting the slider when you need to meet an exact size constraint.

Are my images uploaded to a server?

Never. All compression happens using the browser's built-in Canvas API. Your image is read into browser memory via the File API, processed locally by JavaScript, and written back out as a downloadable Blob, all without any network request. Your image exists only in your browser's memory and is cleared when you close the tab.

How much smaller will my image get?

Typical reductions are 40–80% depending on the original image content and your chosen quality setting. A 3 MB smartphone photo can often drop below 500 KB at 75% quality with no perceptible difference on screen. Images with large areas of uniform color (like backgrounds and logos) tend to compress more aggressively than photographs with high detail.

Why is my PNG getting larger after compression?

PNG is a lossless format, so converting it to a compressed JPG might produce a larger file if the original PNG was already highly optimized. This is more common with small icons, screenshots with flat colors, and graphics with transparent backgrounds. For lossless PNG optimization specifically, consider using a dedicated PNG optimizer. For format conversion, the format converter gives you explicit control over output type.

Privacy Note & Disclaimer

PixMidas is designed to process files directly in your browser whenever possible, and we do not intentionally store or inspect your files. However, no online service can guarantee absolute security, so please avoid highly sensitive data. By using PixMidas, you acknowledge that files are processed at your own discretion and risk.
